summary: Microsoft documents a user-scope Connectivity policy named DisableCrossDeviceResume that disables cross-device resume experiences. The current app instead writes the runtime current-user setting HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\CrossDeviceResume\Configuration\IsResumeAllowed = 0. That observed setting is real, but it is not the same supported control surface as the documented policy, so this record now tracks an implementation mismatch.

setting.name: Disable Cross-Device Resume
setting.area: Policy CSP versus observed user setting
setting.casual_explanation: Resume lets you start on one device and continue on this PC. Microsoft publishes a user-scope policy for disabling that experience, but the app currently writes a separate runtime current-user setting instead.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off when they do not want cross-device handoff behavior for their account. They leave it on when they use Continue on PC or related app handoff flows.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off reduces cross-device convenience for the current user. The key product issue is not whether the feature exists, but that the current app writes a different runtime setting than the official Microsoft-managed surface documented for this behavior.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft Learn documents DisableCrossDeviceResume in Policy CSP - Connectivity as a user-scope policy with default 0 and disabled state 1. Local registry confirms that the app currently writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\CrossDeviceResume\Configuration\IsResumeAllowed = 0 instead. The observed IsResumeAllowed setting may influence the same Settings experience, but this research pass does not treat it as the same supported surface as the documented CSP policy. The CSP page still carries Windows Insider Preview language as of March 14, 2026, so a future implementation alignment should also verify shipping-build applicability before publication.

allowed_values.label: Cross-device resume allowed
allowed_values.meaning: Default policy state. Cross-device resume is not disabled by policy.

allowed_values.label: Cross-device resume disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The user-scope policy disables cross-device resume.

targets.notes: This is the official Microsoft-managed surface captured in this record.

allowed_values.label: No explicit user setting
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses its normal current-user Resume behavior.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: Observed Settings and app value for turning the current-user Resume setting off. This is tracked as a runtime setting, not as the official CSP surface.

allowed_values.label: Observed Resume enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Observed Settings value for turning the current-user Resume setting on.

targets.notes: This is the runtime user setting the app currently writes. This research pass does not treat it as the same supported surface as the documented DisableCrossDeviceResume CSP.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes IsResumeAllowed = 0 under the runtime HKCU CrossDeviceResume configuration key. Microsoft's documented managed surface for this behavior is the user-scope DisableCrossDeviceResume policy CSP, not the runtime setting the app currently changes.

windows_defaults.label: Windows managed default
windows_defaults.applies_to: User profiles where the official DisableCrossDeviceResume policy is not set

states.rationale: Microsoft documents 0 as the default policy value.

windows_defaults.notes: This record treats the documented policy surface as authoritative and keeps the current app runtime setting separate.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: People who want Windows to keep its normal cross-device Resume behavior
avoid_for: People who explicitly want Resume off for the current user

states.rationale: Leave the documented user-scope policy at its default state.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its ordinary cross-device Resume behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You explicitly want to disable cross-device resume.
tradeoffs: Resume stays available where Windows would normally allow it.
The current app avoids publishing an unsupported runtime setting as the official control surface.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Policy CSP - Connectivity / DisableCrossDeviceResume
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents DisableCrossDeviceResume as a user-scope policy with default value 0 and disabled state 1. The page still carries Windows Insider Preview language as of March 14, 2026.

evidence.title: Local PolicyManager default observation
evidence.summary: Local read-only registry query on March 14, 2026 found the PolicyManager default definition for DisableCrossDeviceResume with lowrange 0, highrange 1, and default value 0, confirming the policy contract exists on this build.

evidence.title: Repo Procmon notes for Resume settings
evidence.summary: Repo notes show Settings > Apps > Resume writing IsResumeAllowed = 0 when Resume is off and IsResumeAllowed = 1 when Resume is on.

evidence.title: Local CrossDeviceResume registry observation
evidence.summary: Local read-only registry query on March 14, 2026 found IsResumeAllowed = 0 and IsOneDriveResumeAllowed = 1 under the current-user CrossDeviceResume configuration key, confirming the observed runtime path is present and active on this build.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\CrossDeviceResume\Configuration\IsResumeAllowed = 0 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft now provides a documented user-scope policy for disabling cross-device resume, and the current app does not write that supported surface. It writes a different runtime HKCU setting instead.
blocking_issues: The current app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\CrossDeviceResume\Configuration\IsResumeAllowed = 0 instead of the documented user-scope policy DisableCrossDeviceResume.
The DisableCrossDeviceResume CSP page still carries Windows Insider Preview language as of March 14, 2026, so any future surface alignment should also verify shipping-build applicability.

open_questions: Should the product align to the documented DisableCrossDeviceResume policy surface, or intentionally keep the narrower runtime current-user setting and rename the tweak to match that observed behavior?
If the product aligns to the policy surface, what is the safest supported local implementation path outside MDM management?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: DisableCrossDeviceResume. Scope User. Default Value 0. Format int. 0 = CrossDeviceResume is enabled. 1 = CrossDeviceResume is Disabled.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft directly documents DisableCrossDeviceResume as the supported user-scope policy surface. The current app writes a different runtime HKCU setting instead.
